jokes.text: Jack mentions Liz's memo about including more catchphrases, then delivers an absurd example about leaving in boxers and returning in briefs being 'a dealbreaker, ladies'
jokes.setup: Discussion about catchphrases for the show
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously specific underwear scenario as relationship advice
jokes.explanation: Strong absurdist humor with good specificity. The underwear details make it memorable and quotable.

jokes.text: Wearing an Atlanta Falcons jersey to your sister's wedding as another 'dealbreaker' scenario
jokes.setup: Continuing the dealbreaker catchphrase examples
jokes.punchline: Specific sports team clothing inappropriateness
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up to establish the pattern, though less surprising than the first example.

jokes.text: Jack's over-the-top Italian stereotype impression: 'I'm Jack and I don't know who my father is. I'm very emotional and want to smash these barrels'
jokes.setup: Liz suggests Jack act more Italian instead of repressed Irish
jokes.punchline: Comically exaggerated Italian-American stereotype with barrel-smashing
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Jack's awkwardness with emotion through stereotype performance.

jokes.text: Jack's dark definition of family: 'Resentment, guilt, Easter egg hunts that turn into knife fights'
jokes.setup: Explaining what family means to him
jokes.punchline: Escalating from normal family dysfunction to literal violence
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from mundane to violent, perfectly captures Jack's cynical worldview. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: 'You know what family means to me, Lemon? Grudges, guilt...'
jokes.setup: Jack explains his view of family
jokes.punchline: Dark but realistic take on family dysfunction
jokes.explanation: Classic Jack cynicism with dark humor - very character-specific

jokes.text: Jack wants to whisper something cutting in his mother's ear while she's dying
jokes.setup: Explaining why he won't ask his mother about his father
jokes.punchline: Wanting to get in a final insult on her deathbed
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark humor that perfectly captures Jack's relationship with his mother. Character-specific and memorable.

jokes.text: Liz's Sims reference: when a child doesn't see their father enough, they act erratically and eventually wet themselves
jokes.setup: Liz trying to give Jack family advice
jokes.punchline: Using video game mechanics as psychological insight
jokes.explanation: Clever pop culture reference that works as both character moment and genuine advice analogy.

jokes.text: Liz's sad question: 'Why don't I have any other friends?'
jokes.setup: After giving Jack advice based on video games
jokes.punchline: Self-aware moment of social isolation
jokes.explanation: Good character beat showing Liz's social awkwardness and self-awareness.

jokes.text: Tracy is practicing sitting when Jack enters
jokes.setup: Normal scene entrance
jokes.punchline: Someone needing to practice basic human posture
jokes.explanation: Classic Tracy absurdism - treating basic human function as skill requiring practice.

jokes.text: Tracy's logic about his dead father: if he were alive, he'd be living in Tracy's house taking $200k annually to mow the lawn
jokes.setup: Jack asking why Tracy never looked for his father
jokes.punchline: Assuming any living relative would be financially exploiting him
jokes.explanation: Excellent character-specific logic showing Tracy's cynical view of family and wealth.

jokes.text: Tracy's bizarre guess about Jack's secret: he bought a motorcycle sidecar but his dog refuses to sit in it
jokes.setup: Sensing Jack is hiding something
jokes.punchline: Completely random and specific scenario
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully random Tracy non-sequitur that's oddly specific and memorable.

jokes.text: Tracy's admission: 'It wasn't easy saying that. I struggled to say that sentence'
jokes.setup: After giving Jack heartfelt advice
jokes.punchline: Acknowledging the difficulty of being sincere
jokes.explanation: Good meta moment about Tracy struggling with emotional sincerity.

jokes.text: Tracy's elaborate metaphor about feeling shocked and scared 'like a dog in a sidecar when it detaches from the motorcycle'
jokes.setup: Describing his emotions about finding his son
jokes.punchline: Callback to the earlier random motorcycle sidecar reference
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that recontextualizes the earlier random reference into an emotional metaphor.

jokes.text: Gina's excitement about Brett Michaels wanting to use her catchphrase and wear matching outfits
jokes.setup: Gina discussing her catchphrase's success
jokes.punchline: Over-the-top enthusiasm for minor celebrity endorsement
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Gina's narcissism and low standards for fame.

jokes.text: SportsCenter using the dealbreaker phrase: 'Rangers playing dirty hockey - that's a dealbreaker, ladies!'
jokes.setup: Showing how the catchphrase is spreading
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate application to sports commentary
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing the catchphrase's absurd spread to inappropriate contexts.

jokes.text: 'You know who else likes Deal Breakers? Bret Michaels!'
jokes.setup: Gina excitedly discusses her catchphrase success
jokes.punchline: Random celebrity endorsement
jokes.explanation: Typical Gina celebrity name-dropping - character-appropriate but not particularly clever

jokes.text: Tracy's announcement of reading about his illegitimate son in 'Black Contractors and Butts' magazine
jokes.setup: Making an announcement to the staff
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous fake magazine name combining construction and anatomy
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist magazine name that's specific, unexpected, and memorable.

jokes.text: Tracy repeats the sidecar metaphor again about media hawks discovering the story
jokes.setup: Explaining how the media found out
jokes.punchline: Third use of the specific sidecar metaphor
jokes.explanation: Running the callback into the ground - less effective on third use.

jokes.text: Tracy introduces his son Donald: 'I want you kids to meet a bastard - my little bastard Donald Jordan'
jokes.setup: Introducing his illegitimate son to the staff
jokes.punchline: Playing with literal and figurative meanings of 'bastard'
jokes.explanation: Good wordplay that works both as crude humor and oddly affectionate introduction.

jokes.text: 'Can you give me a thousand dollars for something I need?'
jokes.setup: Donald is introduced as Tracy's son
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks for money
jokes.explanation: Sets up the con artist dynamic immediately - economical character establishment

jokes.text: Len mentions giving his gun to a priest in case he gets depressed again
jokes.setup: Continuing his sad personal updates
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of suicide prevention measure
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered with perfect deadpan timing. Surprisingly heavy topic treated lightly.

jokes.text: I know, but I have a hard time guessing the age of black Americans. Black shoes are the best shoes.
jokes.setup: Discussing Donald's age
jokes.punchline: Kenneth's inappropriate racial comment followed by random shoe observation
jokes.explanation: Cringeworthy in character for Kenneth but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: Liz's 'Mamma Mia' reference and Jack's confusion leading to her explanation of the ABBA musical
jokes.setup: Liz suggesting they handle the three fathers like the movie
jokes.punchline: Jack's complete unfamiliarity with popular culture
jokes.explanation: Good character contrast showing Jack's cultural isolation from mainstream entertainment.

jokes.text: 'It's like the movie Mamma Mia' / 'What?' / 'Nothing, don't push it, let it happen, there'll be a Mamma Mia'
jokes.setup: Liz explains the Mamma Mia connection
jokes.punchline: Her increasingly frantic insistence on the parallel
jokes.explanation: Great escalation of Liz's excitement about the movie parallel - meta and character-driven

jokes.text: 'Listen Tracy, you're 39 years old, right?' / 'Of course, why would I lie about my age? I work in entertainment'
jokes.setup: Liz questions Tracy's age
jokes.punchline: Tracy's illogical reasoning for not lying
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic logic - entertainment industry is exactly where people lie about age

jokes.text: Liz and Kenneth testing Tracy's age with Rob Base's 'It Takes Two' - Tracy performs it perfectly despite claiming to be 39
jokes.setup: Testing if Tracy knows an age-appropriate song
jokes.punchline: He performs it flawlessly, revealing his true age
jokes.explanation: Good setup and payoff showing Tracy accidentally revealing his real age through cultural knowledge.

jokes.text: Donald's birth certificate that Tracy 'printed for him' and leaving a check blank as requested
jokes.setup: Revealing more about the father-son relationship
jokes.punchline: Tracy admits to fabricating documents and giving unlimited money access
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how deep Tracy's enabling goes.

jokes.text: 'And you left the check blank like I asked'
jokes.setup: Donald mentions the check
jokes.punchline: Reveals Tracy gave him a blank check
jokes.explanation: Continues the escalation of the con - shows how easily Tracy is manipulated

jokes.text: 'Are you ready to meet them?' / 'Yes' / 'Jack, meet George Park, he's Korean'
jokes.setup: Liz introduces the potential fathers
jokes.punchline: One of Jack's potential Irish fathers is Korean
jokes.explanation: Perfect misdirection - subverts expectation of Irish fathers with Korean man

jokes.text: Fred's war story about a grenade exploding in his genitals, making his privates look 'like a plate of spaghetti'
jokes.setup: Learning about potential father's ability to reproduce
jokes.punchline: Graphic description of war injury using food metaphor
jokes.explanation: Shockingly graphic and memorable imagery. Dark humor that's both disturbing and funny.

jokes.text: Gina getting a Time Out magazine photoshoot to be named 'Most Whimsical Woman in New York' for no reason
jokes.setup: Discussion of Gina's photo shoot
jokes.punchline: Completely arbitrary and meaningless award
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Gina's narcissism and the arbitrariness of media attention.

jokes.text: Liz's description of her late-night writing process: eating dry bran raisin to stay awake and taking the 'fart train' to work
jokes.setup: Defending her contribution to the writing
jokes.punchline: Gross oversharing about digestive consequences
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment with memorable gross-out imagery that's very Liz-specific.

jokes.text: Liz saving half a muffin 'to eat later' and the observation about what 21-year-old wraps half a muffin
jokes.setup: Watching Donald's behavior
jokes.punchline: Frugal behavior inconsistent with claimed young age
jokes.explanation: Good observational detail that advances the plot while being character-specific.

jokes.text: Kenny's magazine subscription pitch using 'S and M' as abbreviation for 'Magical and Marvelous'
jokes.setup: Donald asking about magazines
jokes.punchline: Innocent reinterpretation of sexual acronym
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing on sexual assumptions and Kenneth's innocence.

jokes.text: Kenneth's casual mention of trying to steal his dead neighbor's identity because 'sometimes things get overwhelming' and he might need to 'get in the car and drive away'
jokes.setup: Explaining why he can't get someone else's birth certificate
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact admission of identity theft plans and escape fantasies
jokes.explanation: Shockingly dark revelation delivered with Kenneth's typical cheerfulness. Excellent character subversion.

jokes.text: 'Don't get me started. My cousin killed everyone at his work'
jokes.setup: Jack asks about family mental illness history
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of mass murder
jokes.explanation: Shocking escalation delivered matter-of-factly - good dark humor

jokes.text: Milton's family description: 'The Greens are very sharp until the very end, which makes the end more terrifying. My father died screaming'
jokes.setup: Discussing family mental health
jokes.punchline: Horrifying family trait delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect buildup and devastating punchline. Very memorable.

jokes.text: 'The Greens are very sharp right up until the end, which makes the end more terrifying. My father died screaming'
jokes.setup: Milton explains family mental health
jokes.punchline: Horrifying detail about his father's death
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark escalation - builds tension then delivers horrifying punchline

jokes.text: Jack's angry outburst: 'Screw you and screw your crappy contest!'
jokes.setup: Political argument reaching its peak
jokes.punchline: Jack losing his composure completely
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Jack's inability to handle disagreement, though not particularly clever.

jokes.text: The photographer suggesting Liz and Gina 'act a little weird' for some shots, leading to prop comedy
jokes.setup: Magazine photo shoot
jokes.punchline: Suggesting deliberate weirdness for manufactured quirkiness
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for photo shoot comedy - functional but not inspired.

jokes.text: The photographer asking Liz to 'imitate a chicken farting' for the shot
jokes.setup: Photo shoot getting more ridiculous
jokes.punchline: Completely absurd and degrading pose request
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of photo shoot absurdity, though relies on crude humor.

jokes.text: Donald's distress call: 'Help mama's baby! Help mama's baby!' while running away
jokes.setup: After supposedly winning the karate fight
jokes.punchline: Childish panic response undermining his tough guy image
jokes.explanation: Good character moment revealing Donald's immaturity despite his con man persona.

jokes.text: Milton's insult about Jack's shoes: asking when his religious cult will commit suicide
jokes.setup: Milton meeting Jack and noticing his style
jokes.punchline: Brutal fashion insult comparing shoes to cult member footwear
jokes.explanation: Sharp, unexpected insult with dark humor. Shows Milton's wit and Jack's fashion missteps.

jokes.text: Discovery that Donald is 40 years old, making him older than Tracy
jokes.setup: Getting Donald's real birth certificate
jokes.punchline: The 'son' is actually older than the 'father'
jokes.explanation: Excellent plot revelation that makes all the previous age confusion pay off.

jokes.text: Tracy's admission: 'Liz Lemon, I may hug people warmly and get lost in malls, but I'm not stupid'
jokes.setup: Liz thinking Tracy is being conned
jokes.punchline: Tracy's self-aware list of his childlike behaviors
jokes.explanation: Great character moment with self-awareness and specific, memorable details about Tracy's quirks.

jokes.text: Tracy comparing Donald to John Travolta's character in 'Grease': attractive, charismatic, and off the straight path
jokes.setup: Explaining why he helps Donald despite knowing he's lying
jokes.punchline: Oddly specific pop culture comparison to a musical character
jokes.explanation: Clever pop culture reference that actually makes sense in context and shows Tracy's genuine affection.

jokes.text: Donald spent Kenneth's chess set money on condoms for the boys at the community center
jokes.setup: Revealing what Donald actually does with the money
jokes.punchline: Using scam money for genuine community service
jokes.explanation: Good twist showing Donald's scam money actually helps people. Recontextualizes the character.

jokes.text: The karate school is named 'Tracy Jordan Karate for Black People'
jokes.setup: Revealing the real name of Donald's business
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily racial business name
jokes.explanation: Typical Tracy absurdist naming convention. Cringe humor that fits the character.

jokes.text: Liz on the magazine cover giving birth to a chicken over a toilet
jokes.setup: The photo shoot results
jokes.punchline: The most embarrassing possible photo was chosen for the cover
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the photo shoot setup. Humiliating and absurd visual that's very memorable.

jokes.text: Magazine cover showing Liz birthing a chicken over a toilet
jokes.setup: Earlier photo shoot sequence
jokes.punchline: The most ridiculous photo made the cover
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to the photo shoot - chose the most embarrassing possible image

jokes.text: Milton's story about not being able to pay rent so he gave Colleen 'something else' - a radio, then later they got drunk and had sex
jokes.setup: Telling the story of meeting Jack's mother
jokes.punchline: Anti-climactic reveal that the 'something else' was just a radio
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that subverts sexual expectations with innocent reality before delivering the actual story.

jokes.text: Milton's immediate request after the father-son reunion: 'I need a kidney transplant'
jokes.setup: Emotional father-son bonding moment
jokes.punchline: Immediate request for major organ donation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy that completely undercuts the sentimental moment. Perfect timing and shocking revelation.
